title: TCP header
summary: The TCP segment header is at least 20 bytes: source and destination ports, a 32-bit sequence number, a 32-bit acknowledgement number, a data-offset/flags word (including SYN/ACK/FIN/RST/PSH/URG), a 16-bit window, checksum, and urgent pointer, followed by optional options padded to a 32-bit boundary.
source_version: RFC 9293 (Transmission Control Protocol), Section 3.1 Header Format

ext.summary: Fixed 20-byte header (plus options). Reliable, ordered, connection-oriented transport. The 4-bit Data Offset gives the header length in 32-bit words so a parser knows where options end and payload begins.

structure.meaning: Byte-stream sequence number of the first data octet (or the ISN+1 if SYN set).
Next sequence number the sender expects, valid when the ACK flag is set.
Header length in 32-bit words (5 = 20 bytes minimum, up to 15 = 60 bytes with options).
Must be zero (formerly part of a 6-bit reserved + flags layout).
CWR, ECE, URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN control bits.
Receive window: bytes the sender of this segment is willing to accept (scaled by the Window Scale option if negotiated).
Ones-complement checksum over the header, payload, and a pseudo-header.
Offset of urgent data, valid when the URG flag is set.
Optional, e.g. MSS, Window Scale, SACK-Permitted, Timestamps; padded with zeros to a 32-bit boundary.

ext.example_decoded: First two bytes 0x01BB = 443 as a destination port (HTTPS). Full segment parsing requires the IP header for length/pseudo-header checksum.
notes: Minimum header is 20 bytes; Data Offset < 5 is invalid.
The checksum is computed over a pseudo-header (src/dst IP, protocol=6, TCP length) plus the TCP header and data.
SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK is the three-way handshake; FIN or RST tears the connection down.
